blocks.content: When the crying stops, it doesn't stop all at once. It stops in waves — subsiding and returning and subsiding again, the way things that have been held for a long time don't release cleanly.

She pulls back. She presses the napkin from her pocket to her face — she had tucked it there that morning, without knowing why, the way you sometimes prepare for things before you're conscious of preparing.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: I'm crying on a Ferris wheel.
Her voice is wrecked. She says it to the space between them, not quite looking at him.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: That's not what I'd call it.
She looks at him. He looks at her — eyes still red, face completely open, not a single thing in it that she needs to interpret or manage or worry about.
Small, and wet, and surprised. But real.

She laughs again. She presses the napkin to her face one more time. She breathes. She breathes again. Slow, full breaths — the body finding its way back to normal, the way it always does, the faithful machinery of her doing what it's always done.
She is here. She is still here.
